name: Aashish Sommaiya
role: Equity Partner, WhiteOak Capital Group; ED & CEO, WhiteOak Capital Asset
quote: Most investors don't lose to bad funds, they lose to fund-switching.
bio_html:
CEO of WhiteOak Capital. Long-term, valuation-disciplined investor; explains the many paths to financial heaven.

In their own words

Most investors don't lose to bad funds, they lose to fund-switching.

What they care about

Aashish Sommaiya, equity partner at WhiteOak Capital Group and CEO of WhiteOak Capital Asset, keeps pulling investors back to a simpler first question: not which fund to buy, but why you are investing at all. His argument is that the goal decides the vehicle, that there is no single perfect road to wealth, and that staying inside regulated products beats chasing whatever the market is loudest about.

What a listener takes away is that outcomes turn less on macroeconomics and more on how you handle your own emotions.
excerpt: CEO of WhiteOak Capital. Long-term, valuation-disciplined investor; explains the many paths to financial heaven.

name: Abhinav Angirish
quote: A house you live in is a lifestyle decision, not an investment.
bio_html:
Founder of Investonline.in. Demystifies real estate vs equity, and the emotional side of buying a house.

In their own words

A house you live in is a lifestyle decision, not an investment.

What they care about

Abhinav argues that buying a home is never one-size-fits-all: the right call turns on your life stage, your cash flow and your financial readiness, not on what worked for someone else. He is candid about what first-time buyers miss, the illiquidity of property and the hidden charges beyond the sticker price, and he insists a house should fit inside a diversified portfolio rather than swallow it.

The takeaway for listeners: renting while you get ready is a sound, flexible choice, not a failure, and financial planning comes before the purchase.
excerpt: Founder of Investonline.in. Demystifies real estate vs equity, and the emotional side of buying a house.

name: Ajit Menon
quote: Everyone can be happy; it is the constant chase to be happier where the trouble usually starts.
bio_html:
Investment strategist focused on long-term wealth building strategies for Indian investors.

In their own words

Everyone can be happy; it is the constant chase to be happier where the trouble usually starts.

What they care about

Ajit Menon, former CEO of PGIM Mutual Fund, brings 30 years to one reframe: retirement planning is really about financial freedom, security and peace of mind, and that shift changes the decisions you make. His core theme is happy versus happier, the idea that the constant chase to feel better is where the trouble usually begins.

He walks through all seven asset classes and warns against concentrating in one while ignoring economic cycles, favouring simple rules like buying at a discount and keeping things uncomplicated with Balance Advantage Funds. For anyone investing on a limited income, he is honest about sequence of return risk and realistic expectations, so the anxiety that stops people from planning does not get the last word.
excerpt: Investment strategist focused on long-term wealth building strategies for Indian investors.

name: Amit Bivalkar
quote: The industry sells products. Your job is to buy outcomes.
bio_html:
Founder of Sapient Wealth Advisors & Brokers. Pune-based wealth manager with two decades of advising HNIs through cycles. Outspoken on the gap between what the financial industry sells and what investors actually need.

What we'll explore

Add a few notes here on what this conversation will cover. This text appears on the guest profile page on the public website.
excerpt: Founder of Sapient Wealth Advisors & Brokers. Pune-based wealth manager with two decades of advising HNIs through cycles. Outspoken on the gap between what the …

name: CA Anuj Jain
quote: Volatility is not risk. Risk is owning a business you never took the time to understand.
bio_html:
Tell us about this guest…

In their own words

Volatility is not risk. Risk is owning a business you never took the time to understand.

What they care about

Anuj Jain is a chartered accountant and co-founder of Green Portfolio whose family has held equity for three generations, choosing stocks over gold and real estate. His core argument is that volatility is not the same as risk: what actually protects you is understanding the business you own, because even a largecap can be dangerous when you do not.

In his episode he works through smallcaps, PMS and mutual funds while watching promoter ethics, balance sheet discipline and the red flags that expose a weak company. The takeaway for a listener is that equity rewards temperament far more than timing.
excerpt: Tell us about this guest…

name: Anupam Tiwari
role: Head of Equities, Groww Mutual Fund
quote: The fund's return and the investor's return are two different numbers, almost always.
bio_html:
Heads the equity desk at Groww Mutual Fund. Brings the perspective of a fund manager building portfolios for India's largest new-age investor base, what works, what doesn't, and why most retail investors never actually capture the returns their funds deliver.

In their own words

The fund's return and the investor's return are two different numbers, almost always.

What they care about

Anupam Tiwari, Head of Equities at Groww Mutual Fund, spends his time separating signal from noise. He argues India is structurally far stronger than it was 20 years ago, that FII selling reads better as redemption pressure than a vote against the country, and that investing abroad carries a real cost rather than being an obvious upgrade.

A listener comes away with steadier return expectations, a clearer way to weigh active against passive, and the case for discipline and a written plan.
excerpt: Heads the equity desk at Groww Mutual Fund. Brings the perspective of a fund manager building portfolios for India's largest new-age investor base, what works, …

name: Bhalchandra Joshi
quote: The cycle you can't sit through is the cycle you can't profit from.
bio_html:
Three decades of mutual fund experience. On the show with the unsexy lesson nobody wants to hear: SIP works. He's seen it. The data backs it. The only thing that goes wrong is the investor.

In their own words

The cycle you can't sit through is the cycle you can't profit from.

What they care about

Bhalchandra Joshi has spent more than thirty years building India's mutual fund industry, and as COO of The Wealth Company he speaks from what he has lived through rather than theory: real crashes, real bull runs, real investor panic. His case is plain: SIPs keep working, and the hard part is not starting one but continuing it when markets fall.

He watches investors repeat the same mistakes at each correction, and points out that even seasoned experts lean on an advisor, so going it alone is not automatically the smarter route. The takeaway is quieter than any tip: patience and a sensible number of funds, not constant activity, are what build wealth over decades.
excerpt: Three decades of mutual fund experience. On the show with the unsexy lesson nobody wants to hear: SIP works. He's seen it. The data backs it. The only thing tha…

name: Chirag Barjatya
bio_html:
Founder of PFC (Personal Finance Coach). Pushes the inconvenient idea that wealth without health is meaningless.

In their own words

name: Debashish Bose
quote: A stuck feeling is not the same as a broken plan, valuations simply have to compress first.
bio_html:
Founder of Infinite Circle. Brings a global macro lens to Indian markets, covers cross-border investing, bond-equity dynamics, and dollar flows.

In their own words

A stuck feeling is not the same as a broken plan, valuations simply have to compress first.

What they care about

Debashish Bose founded Infinite Circle after stepping away from managing money in 2024, when a roughly fourfold jump in shareholder counts convinced him IPOs had become a tool for distribution rather than value creation. He argues India's market cap sitting near twice its GDP is hard to justify, and that valuations have to compress, sometimes for years, before the next real leg up.

For a listener whose portfolio has felt stuck for months, his point is that a stuck feeling is not the same as a broken plan. He ties the local picture to a wider story of debt behind tariffs and wars and a derivatives market roughly twenty times GDP, then lands on the practical question of what sensible asset allocation looks like in 2026.
excerpt: Founder of Infinite Circle. Brings a global macro lens to Indian markets, covers cross-border investing, bond-equity dynamics, and dollar flows.

name: Devina Mehra
quote: Betting only on India can quietly cap your wealth, so spread risk across countries, currencies and assets.
bio_html:
Founder of First Global. Among India's most-quoted contrarian investors on why Indian-only portfolios are risky.

In their own words

Betting only on India can quietly cap your wealth, so spread risk across countries, currencies and assets.

What they care about

Devina Mehra, founder of First Global, argues that good investing is meant to be boring: asset allocation, diversification and risk management doing the quiet work in the background, not clever stock tips. She pushes back on the small-cap and hot-IPO chase, pointing out that most small caps do not survive, and on the comfort of betting only on India, making the case for spreading risk across countries, currencies and assets.

Her recurring theme is that your brain is not wired for markets, so sticking to a system tends to beat reacting to emotion or noise. A listener leaves questioning the investing truths they have quietly absorbed, including the assumption that they are automatically their own best fund manager.
excerpt: Founder of First Global. Among India's most-quoted contrarian investors on why Indian-only portfolios are risky.

name: Dhirendra Kumar
quote: Don't be a fund collector: three or four funds, disciplined SIPs, and the noise tuned out.
bio_html:
CEO of Value Research. India's most-read mutual fund analyst, and a 7-figure-view ThisOrThat episode.

In their own words

Don't be a fund collector: three or four funds, disciplined SIPs, and the noise tuned out.

What they care about

Dhirendra Kumar, CEO of Value Research, has spent 30 years arguing that investing is simpler than it looks. On the show he pushes back against over-engineering your portfolio: three or four funds, regular SIPs, and the discipline to ignore market and social media noise are what build lasting wealth in India, not clever timing.

He treats equity, fixed income, and insurance as separate jobs, with asset allocation there mainly to manage risk. A listener leaves with the everyday calls sorted: how much to hold in equity, when to exit a fund, SIP versus lumpsum, and direct versus regular plans.
excerpt: CEO of Value Research. India's most-read mutual fund analyst, and a 7-figure-view ThisOrThat episode.

name: Kalpen Parekh
quote: Buy good and hold it, and if you buy bad, do not hold even for a day.
bio_html:
Tell us about this guest…

In their own words

Buy good and hold it, and if you buy bad, do not hold even for a day.

What they care about

Kalpen Parekh, CEO of DSP Mutual Fund, argues that 'which fund should I buy?' is almost always the wrong question, because it skips the personal context, your time horizon, risk appetite and current portfolio, that decides whether a fund actually fits you. His real subject is behaviour, not knowledge: Indian investors keep losing money even when they already know the right theory.

He boils good investing down to three rules, quality, valuation and time horizon: buy good and hold, and if you buy bad, do not hold even for a day. With markets expensive, equities in a correction and gold at all-time highs, he leans on multi-asset investing precisely because you cannot know in advance what will win.
excerpt: Tell us about this guest…

name: Karan Datta
bio_html:
Chief Business Officer at Axis Mutual Fund. Guest on This or That on silver, time vs money, and wealth psychology.

In their own words

Add a memorable quote from this guest's episode here. Use the > markdown to render it as a pull quote.

name: Kirtan Shah
quote: For 99% of investors, global investing is FOMO, not strategy; a disciplined India portfolio does the work.
bio_html:
Founder of Credence Wealth and one of India's most-followed CFPs. Guest on This or That on global investing and freedom-through-wealth.

In their own words

For 99% of investors, global investing is FOMO, not strategy; a disciplined India portfolio does the work.

What they care about

Kirtan Shah, founder of Credence Wealth, treats financial planning as far more than picking investments: it starts with managing risk, an emergency fund and taxes, and only then builds the portfolio. He is blunt that most of the online global-investing buzz is FOMO, and argues that for the vast majority of retail investors a disciplined, India-focused allocation is still the sensible path.

His recurring point is that asset allocation is behavioral management meant to keep you steady, not just cap risk, and that your plan should shift with your life stage. A listener leaves with a calmer checklist: buy term insurance, keep investing toward clear goals, resist chasing last year's winner, and remember to actually enjoy the life you are saving for.
excerpt: Founder of Credence Wealth and one of India's most-followed CFPs. Guest on This or That on global investing and freedom-through-wealth.

name: Krishna Sharma
bio_html:
Founder of Gateway to Financial Freedom. Frequent ThisOrThat guest on gold, investor behaviour, and fund selection.

In their own words

Add a memorable quote from this guest's episode here. Use the > markdown to render it as a pull quote.

What they care about

Write a few paragraphs about this guest's core ideas, recurring themes across their appearances, and what listeners take away. Markdown supported, use ## for headings, bold, italic, > for quotes.
excerpt: Founder of Gateway to Financial Freedom. Frequent ThisOrThat guest on gold, investor behaviour, and fund selection.

name: Lisa Pallavi Barbora
quote: Build awareness of your own numbers first, and every money conversation after that gets more honest.
bio_html:
Financial journalist with a special interest in women and money. On the show on taking financial charge.

In their own words

Build awareness of your own numbers first, and every money conversation after that gets more honest.

What they care about

Lisa Pallavi Barbora, a financial coach and author of Money and Her, argues that women's hesitation with money is rarely about ability: it is fear of numbers, guilt, and the weight of family responsibility, often layered with self-doubt about whether they can even learn it. Her fix is plain and doable: know your own five simple numbers first, use the 50-30-20 rule as a starting frame, and secure the foundations of an emergency fund, health insurance, and term life before shifting from saving to investing.

The takeaway a listener leaves with is that taking charge begins with a mindset shift and a willingness to learn, and that understanding your own numbers is what makes money talks with a partner easier and more honest.
excerpt: Financial journalist with a special interest in women and money. On the show on taking financial charge.

name: Mahavir Chopra
quote: A policy should protect you, not invest for you, so never let one product quietly try to do both.
bio_html:
Founder of Beshak.org. Speaks on health and life insurance, why most people are mis-sold both.

In their own words

A policy should protect you, not invest for you, so never let one product quietly try to do both.

What they care about

Mahavir Chopra, founder of Beshak.org, wants Indians to buy insurance for one job: protection. Across his episodes he keeps protection and investing apart, favouring plain term cover over bundled ULIPs and endowments, and pushing you to own a health policy that stays with you through a job change, a layoff or a diagnosis rather than leaning on corporate cover.

He is unusually practical about the fine print: fill the proposal form honestly because it is legally binding, and judge an insurer by its complaints ratio and network hospitals, not the claim settlement ratio alone. A listener leaves seeing insurance as risk cover rather than a savings scheme, and knowing the smart choices are the careful ones made at the buying stage.
excerpt: Founder of Beshak.org. Speaks on health and life insurance, why most people are mis-sold both.

name: Mahmood Basha Shaik
quote: A debt fund is not a fixed deposit. Pretending otherwise is how people get hurt.
bio_html:
Fund manager at Baroda BNP Paribas Mutual Fund. Spoke on This or That about global investing and the NASDAQ-vs-Nifty debate.

In their own words

Add a memorable quote from this guest's episode here. Use the > markdown to render it as a pull quote.

name: Mohit Bhatia
quote: Investing is simple to describe but hard to live: your behaviour, not the market, builds your wealth.
bio_html:
Tell us about this guest…

In their own words

Investing is simple to describe but hard to live: your behaviour, not the market, builds your wealth.

What they care about

Mohit Bhatia keeps returning to one uncomfortable truth: the hard part of investing is not knowing the rules, it is living by them. He separates an information edge, an analytical edge and a behavioural edge, and argues the behavioural one decides most outcomes, since investor returns rarely match market returns.

A listener leaves with practical framing: sensible diversification beats owning 10 to 20 random funds, asset allocation plus tools like SIP, STP and SWP can turn volatility into a friend, and a good advisor earns their value mostly by managing behaviour and simplifying choices.
excerpt: Tell us about this guest…

name: Mrin Agarwal
quote: Financial freedom is control over your time and meeting your goals, not a number chased for its own sake.
bio_html:
30 years of experience in wealth management and financial education and inclusion. Her passion is to empower millions of working Indians by providing them the tools needed to manage their personal finance and be financially independent.

In their own words

Financial freedom is control over your time and meeting your goals, not a number chased for its own sake.

What they care about

Mrin Agarwal, founder of Finsafe, has educated over 300,000 investors, and she reframes financial freedom as control over your time and enough to meet your goals rather than a number chased for its own sake. Her guidance is practical and unhyped: start early so compounding has years to work, know your own risk profile, and diversify instead of chasing last year's returns.

A listener leaves seeing equity funds and NPS as the long-term growth engine and debt funds as the safety net for emergency money, with a step-up SIP as a realistic path for those who started late.
excerpt: 30 years of experience in wealth management and financial education and inclusion. Her passion is to empower millions of working Indians by providing them the t…

name: Neil Borate
quote: India is 4% of global markets, so an all-India portfolio isn't diversification, it's a concentrated bet.
bio_html:
Deputy Editor at Mint. One of India's clearest writers on personal finance, mutual funds, and global investing.

In their own words

India is 4% of global markets, so an all-India portfolio isn't diversification, it's a concentrated bet.

What they care about

Neil Borate, Deputy Editor at Mint, makes the case that investing is simpler than the industry suggests: for about 90% of people a plain core-satellite mix of mutual funds is enough, and PMS, AIF and 20% return dreams are mostly distractions. His steady refrain is that behaviour, not the product you pick, decides how you actually do.

Where he pushes further is global investing: India is roughly 4% of world markets, so an India-only portfolio sits out whole engines of profit like AI, defence and commodities. A listener leaves knowing to keep it simple while building, and once the India base is serious, to weigh a global allocation with clear eyes about the routes and costs, from LRS to Gift City.
excerpt: Deputy Editor at Mint. One of India's clearest writers on personal finance, mutual funds, and global investing.

name: Nimesh Chandan
quote: Behaviour, not knowledge, separates good investors from great ones.
bio_html:
Chief Investment Officer at Bajaj Finserv AMC. Known for his sharp framework on behavioural finance, the gap between what investors should do and what they actually do, and his writing on market cycles, valuations, and decision-making under uncertainty.

What we'll explore

Add a few notes here on what this conversation will cover. This text appears on the guest profile page on the public website.
excerpt: Chief Investment Officer at Bajaj Finserv AMC. Known for his sharp framework on behavioural finance, the gap between what investors should do and what they actu…

name: Niranjan Avasthi
quote: Behaviour beats the best fund: knowing when to exit matters more than owning the top performer.
bio_html:
Head of Product at Edelweiss Mutual Fund. On the show with a foundational primer on mutual fund investing for first-time investors.

In their own words

Behaviour beats the best fund: knowing when to exit matters more than owning the top performer.

What they care about

Niranjan Avasthi, Head of Product at Edelweiss Mutual Fund, is out to make fund investing feel less overwhelming. Faced with 36-plus categories, he hands listeners two plain frameworks: T.I.M.E. to choose your first funds, and F.I.T. to judge the fund house, team and track record instead of chasing whatever performed well recently.

His recurring point is that behaviour beats performance: rolling returns tell a more honest story than cherry-picked point-to-point numbers, and knowing when to exit and staying steady matter more than owning the single best fund. A listener leaves with a simple 80:20 equity-debt starting formula and a clear-eyed take on SIPs, NFOs, gold and thematic funds.
excerpt: Head of Product at Edelweiss Mutual Fund. On the show with a foundational primer on mutual fund investing for first-time investors.

name: Pooja Bhinde
quote: Chasing returns is the wrong start: wealth is psychology, discipline and a plan you keep raising.
bio_html:
Financial planner who turned a step-up SIP into a ₹10 crore corpus. Strong views on money psychology.

In their own words

Chasing returns is the wrong start: wealth is psychology, discipline and a plan you keep raising.

What they care about

Pooja Bhinde is a Certified Financial Planner who treats wealth as a question of psychology, discipline and planning rather than hot tips or the next product. She keeps returning to the step-up SIP, where you raise your contribution as your income grows, and to the wider plan around it: budgeting, cash flow, a contingency fund, insurance and staying clear of debt traps.

Her stories run from a house help's SIP to security for senior citizens, and she is firm that women belong in a household's money decisions. A listener leaves seeing wealth-building as a habit almost anyone can start, not a game reserved for the rich.
excerpt: Financial planner who turned a step-up SIP into a ₹10 crore corpus. Strong views on money psychology.

name: Raghav Iyengar
quote: Staying steady when markets swing is a skill you practice, not a temperament you were born with.
bio_html:
CEO of Axis Mutual Fund. Veteran of the Indian asset management industry.

In their own words

Staying steady when markets swing is a skill you practice, not a temperament you were born with.

What they care about

Raghav Iyengar brings over three decades in the mutual fund industry, and what he cares about most is how investor behavior has shifted: from short-term trading to goal-based investing.

His discipline is concrete: aim to save at least 30% of your monthly income, build an emergency fund before you reach for anything riskier, stick with regulated investment managers, and get honest about your own risk profile. The idea that stays with a listener is that staying calm through volatile markets is a practiced skill, not a personality trait, and it matters as much as which fund you pick.
excerpt: CEO of Axis Mutual Fund. Veteran of the Indian asset management industry.

name: Shaymali Basu
quote: Your own fear, greed and FOMO do more damage to your wealth than any market move.
bio_html:
Financial planner focused on the emotional side of wealth-building, why most investing decisions are about psychology, not math.

In their own words

Your own fear, greed and FOMO do more damage to your wealth than any market move.

What they care about

Shaymali Basu spent more than 30 years at HDFC Mutual Fund, and she keeps returning to one idea: the biggest threat to your wealth is not the market, it is your own FOMO, herd mentality, fear and greed. She makes the case for goal-based investing, where every rupee is tied to a purpose, over trying to time the market, and for letting your asset allocation shift as you move through your 20s, 30s and 40s.

Her cautions are practical. Over-diversification and investing at random quietly hurt you, so a focused portfolio you actually review beats a scattered one you forget, and starting late is not a dead end when you build the saving habit and attach real goals to your money.
excerpt: Financial planner focused on the emotional side of wealth-building, why most investing decisions are about psychology, not math.

name: Sneha Jain
quote: A SIP is only the pipe, not the investment: know the fund before you commit.
bio_html:
Fund manager. Explains why SIP and equity investing remain misunderstood by most retail investors.

In their own words

A SIP is only the pipe, not the investment: know the fund before you commit.

What they care about

Sneha Jain, a fund manager and CEO of Wealth Trust, keeps pulling investors back to one distinction: the method is not the investment. A SIP is just the pipe, so what matters is the fund underneath it, and on equity she argues for mutual funds as the base of a portfolio, around 60 to 65 percent, with direct stocks kept as a smaller, disciplined satellite you actually have time to research.

On retirement she is just as plain-spoken, asking you to face the real cost early, size your corpus against inflation and healthcare rather than today's expenses, and review the plan as life changes. A listener leaves knowing what to own, why starting early is the edge you cannot buy back, and how to keep a plan honest over time.
excerpt: Fund manager. Explains why SIP and equity investing remain misunderstood by most retail investors.

name: Swaroop Mohanty
quote: Buying low is easy. Holding through low is the hard part.
bio_html:
CEO of Mirae Asset Mutual Fund, one of India's most-respected fund houses, built on consistency rather than marketing. Long-standing advocate for low-cost, long-horizon investing and for transparent fund management.

What we'll explore

Add a few notes here on what this conversation will cover. This text appears on the guest profile page on the public website.
excerpt: CEO of Mirae Asset Mutual Fund, one of India's most-respected fund houses, built on consistency rather than marketing. Long-standing advocate for low-cost, long…

name: Thomas Priju
quote: Judge a fund manager over five years, not a couple of underwhelming quarters.
bio_html:
Tell us about this guest…

In their own words

Judge a fund manager over five years, not a couple of underwhelming quarters.

What they care about

Thomas Priju is a fund manager with more than 25 years across mutual funds, PMS, hedge funds and AIFs, and he talks about how managers actually think rather than how they pitch. His recurring themes are the honest ones: alpha is the real engine of returns, but stock picking is genuinely hard, which is why sector cycles and business inflection points carry so much weight, and why an oversubscribed IPO of a loss-making company should make you pause.

The takeaway for a listener is patience over reaction: give a manager five years rather than a few quarters, and weigh process, humility and temperament more than any single clever call.
excerpt: Tell us about this guest…

name: Vijai Mantri
quote: If you can hold for seven years, equity is the answer; if you can't, it isn't the question.
bio_html:
Veteran asset manager and founder of Vijai Mantri Financial Services. Recurring guest on This or That with sharp views on Indian markets, family businesses, and behavioural investing.

In their own words

If you can hold for seven years, equity is the answer; if you can't, it isn't the question.

What they care about

Vijai Mantri, founder of VMFS, keeps returning to one uncomfortable truth: the return a fund earns and the return an investor keeps are two different numbers, and behaviour is what widens or closes the gap. He points out that Indians will hold gold for 30 years yet sell equity within three, and that impatience, not a lack of intelligence, is what quietly breaks compounding.

He reframes a falling NAV as a SIP investor's friend, favours productive assets over a metal sitting in a locker, and says a real advisor leads with risk and mentions returns last. The takeaway is less about picking cleverer funds and more about building a process you can actually hold through the noise.
excerpt: Veteran asset manager and founder of Vijai Mantri Financial Services. Recurring guest on This or That with sharp views on Indian markets, family businesses, and…

name: Vinayak Sapre
quote: Your temperament, not your spreadsheet, decides your money, and Kabir and Rahim knew that before Excel did.
bio_html:
Financial coach with a quiet, disciplined approach to wealth-building. Built financial freedom at 40.

In their own words

Your temperament, not your spreadsheet, decides your money, and Kabir and Rahim knew that before Excel did.

What they care about

Vinayak Sapre is a financial coach who reached financial freedom in 2011 and has argued ever since that money is mostly a temperament problem, not a products or Excel-sheet one. He leans on Kabir and Rahim's dohas to link old wisdom to modern decisions, reframes retirement as FILE, financial independence with life at ease, and warns that chasing the biggest returns usually ends in disappointment.

A listener comes away seeing a good advisor as a filter, a saadhu jaisa soop that keeps only what belongs in your portfolio, and remembering that the point is to enjoy your money, not just accumulate it, even if you are starting late.
excerpt: Financial coach with a quiet, disciplined approach to wealth-building. Built financial freedom at 40.
